summary: Applied the shared Keycloak SSO broker live on docker-runtime-lv3, provisioned its PostgreSQL backend on postgres-lv3, published it at sso.lv3.org, wired Grafana through OIDC, and verified the approved agent client-credentials path.

verification.observed: HETZNER_DNS_API_TOKEN=... make converge-keycloak completed successfully from the integration worktree across Hetzner DNS, postgres-lv3, docker-runtime-lv3, nginx-lv3, and monitoring-lv3.
dig +short @1.1.1.1 sso.lv3.org A returned 65.108.75.123 after the shared edge certificate expanded to include sso.lv3.org.
curl --resolve sso.lv3.org:443:65.108.75.123 returned the lv3 realm OpenID configuration with issuer https://sso.lv3.org/realms/lv3.
POST to /realms/lv3/protocol/openid-connect/token with client_id=lv3-agent-hub and the controller-local secret returned a bearer access_token.
curl -I https://grafana.lv3.org/login/generic_oauth returned a 302 redirect to the Keycloak authorization endpoint with client_id=grafana-oauth.

uv run --with requests --with python-socketio --with websocket-client python scripts/uptime_kuma_tool.py ensure-monitors created the Keycloak OIDC Discovery monitor and left the other managed monitors updated.

notes: During rollout the Hetzner DNS reconcile hit a censored update failure while normalizing legacy A record TTLs. The existing public A records for proxmox, grafana, nginx, docker, and build were normalized to TTL 60 directly through the Hetzner API and the sso A record was created, after which the repo-managed converge reran successfully.

The controller's recursive DNS cache still lagged immediately after the apply, so public verification used the authoritative 1.1.1.1 resolver and direct SNI verification against 65.108.75.123 until local cache propagation caught up.
